Completed sw-checklist validation confirming Saga 14 step 002 implementation stays within project complexity budgets. Both mlpl-rt and mlpl-mlx pass all hard limits with warnings appearing at designed growth thresholds. The modular organization strategy succeeded - splitting primitives across activations, elementwise, shapes, and transforms modules keeps each under the 7-function maximum while enabling the expanded primitive surface. Warnings indicate healthy growth: mlpl-rt's 5-function modules and 6-module count, mlpl-mlx's 6-function modules and 27-line matmul function all remain below hard caps. The architecture described in documentation ("each primitive is in its own module so the crate stays under the sw-checklist function-count budget") proved effective. The mlpl-mlx without-feature test correctly runs 0 tests (tests require mlx feature), and mlpl-parity-tests passes its 2 speedup measurement tests. Overall workspace shows 101 passed checks with warnings concentrated in test-heavy crates, not the runtime implementations.

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, use Config tool.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (triggers) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/triggers.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- mw-cp: Mike Wright's Checkpoint Process — pre-commit checks, doc updates, detailed commit, and push
- claude-mem:do: Execute a plan using subagents for implementation
- claude-mem:make-plan: Create an implementation plan with documentation discovery
- claude-mem:mem-search: Search claude-mem's persistent cross-session memory database. Use when user asks "did we already solve this?", "how did we do X last time?", or needs work from previous sessions.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
=== PLAN ===
# MLX Backend Milestone (Saga 14, v0.11.0)

## Why this exists

Saga 13 proved the platform thesis on CPU: a few lines of MLPL
train a tiny transformer LM, generate text, and render its own
attention. The next bottleneck is speed. The Saga 13 Tiny LM
trains at interpreter pace on an M-class laptop; an accelerator
backend is the proper lever.

Apple Silicon is this project's baseline dev hardware, and MLX
is its native array framework: lazy graph execution, automatic
operator fusion, unified CPU+GPU memory, and no kernel-per-device
dance. Its array semantics already agree with MLPL's
`DenseArray` (row-major, shape-labeled, consistent broadcast
rules). Saga 14 introduces MLX as MLPL's first accelerator
backend behind the same source surface: a program that trains on
CPU should train on MLX without source changes.

CUDA (Saga 17) is the second accelerator backend. The MLPL
source surface shipped in Saga 14 is the same surface CUDA will
land behind. See `docs/using-mlx.md` for the user-facing design
sketch; this doc is the implementation plan.

## Non-goals

- **No CUDA.** Saga 17.
- **No distributed execution.** Saga 17.
- **No LoRA / quantization.** Saga 15.
- **No new Model DSL layers.** The existing `linear`, `chain`,
  `residual`, `rms_norm`, `attention`, `causal_attention`,
  `embed`, `sinusoidal_encoding`, activations, and
  `cross_entropy` ship on MLX as-is.
- **No checkpoint format.** Still deferred to Saga 15+.
- **No pretraining claims.** Target is the Saga 13 Tiny LM demo
  running faster, not a bigger model.

## Quality requirements (every step)

Identical to Saga 13:

1. TDD: failing test first, then implementation, then refactor.
2. Quality gates must all pass before commit:
   - `cargo test`
   - `cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings`
   - `cargo fmt --all` + `cargo fmt --all -- --check`
   - `markdown-checker -f "**/*.md"` if docs touched
   - `sw-checklist`
3. Use `/mw-cp` checkpoint process.
4. Push immediately after commit.
5. Web UI changes rebuild `pages/` via `scripts/build-pages.sh`.
6. `.agentrail/` changes are committed whenever they change.
7. MLX-feature tests are gated so CI on non-Apple-Silicon hosts
   still passes (`#[cfg(all(target_os = "macos",
   target_arch = "aarch64", feature = "mlx"))]` or the closest
   equivalent supported by `mlx-rs`). Where MLX is unavailable,
   the CPU path remains authoritative.

## What already exists

- `mlpl-rt` runtime target (scalar, array, fncall, variable,
  labels, matmul with static contraction check) used by both
  `mlpl!` proc macro and `mlpl-build` CLI.
- `mlpl-parity-tests` harness proving bit-for-bit agreement
  between interpreter and compile-to-Rust paths on 9 programs.
- `mlpl-bench` Criterion harness comparing interpreter vs
  compiled MLPL (9.05x measured speedup on 100x100 reshape+
  reduce).
- `mlpl-lower-rs` path-configurable AST -> `TokenStream` codegen
  (the `runtime_prefix` knob makes swapping runtimes cheap).
- Full Saga 11/13 Model DSL, autograd, optimizers, and the
  Tiny LM end-to-end demo (`demos/tiny_lm.mlpl`).
- Labeled axes and structured `EvalError::ShapeMismatch`
  propagate through every existing op.

## Phase 1 -- mlpl-mlx runtime target (3 steps)

### Step 001 -- mlpl-mlx crate skeleton + first primitive
New `crates/mlpl-mlx` sibling to `mlpl-rt`. `mlx-rs` dependency
behind a `mlx` Cargo feature. Ports exactly one primitive --
`matmul(a, b)` -- backed by MLX's `matmul` and returning an
MLX array wrapped in the same `DenseArray` / `LabeledShape`
shape as `mlpl-rt`. Parity test vs `mlpl-rt::matmul` on a
`[8, 4] @ [4, 8]` fixture (bit-for-bit or within a documented
fp32 tolerance; decide during TDD). `cargo test -p mlpl-mlx
--features mlx` gates the test; default build stays green on
non-Apple hosts.

### Step 002 -- core elementwise + shape ops on MLX
Port the shape- and element-level primitives the existing
interpreter uses most: `add`/`sub`/`mul`/`div`/`neg`, `exp`/
`log`/`tanh`/`sigmoid`/`relu`, `reshape`, `transpose`. Each
primitive lands with a unit test + parity test vs `mlpl-rt`.
Labels propagate identically -- the MLX runtime does not own
`LabeledShape`, it borrows `mlpl-core`'s. Gradcheck is not in
scope yet (step 006).

### Step 003 -- reductions + softmax + log-softmax on MLX
`reduce_add`/`reduce_mul`/`mean`/`argmax` (axis-aware, label-
aware), `softmax`, `log_softmax`, and `cross_entropy`.
Numerical-stability invariants from the CPU path (max-
subtraction log-softmax) carry over. Parity tests on `[4, 3]`
and `[2, 3, 5]` fixtures. At the end of this phase the MLX
runtime can compute forward passes of every primitive the Tiny
LM uses.

## Phase 2 -- Device placement + backend dispatch (2 steps)

### Step 004 -- device("...") scoped form + parser surface
New `device("mlx") { body }` scoped form in the parser/AST
(`Expr::Device`), mirroring `experiment`'s shape. Inside the
block, evaluator dispatches array allocations and ops through
the MLX runtime when the `mlx` feature is enabled; falls back
to CPU (with a one-time warning) otherwise. Labels and shapes
propagate identically across the boundary. Tests: parser
round-trip, evaluator returns the same shape+labels as the CPU
path, `device("cpu") { }` is a no-op and works on every host.

### Step 005 -- backend dispatch in the Model DSL
`apply(model, X)` and `params(model)` route through the active
backend when `X` was allocated inside a `device("mlx")` block.
A model owns its parameters on one device -- moving between
devices requires an explicit `to_device("cpu")` / `to_device
("mlx")` builtin (single-direction helpers that copy tensors;
tests cover round-trip equality). The Saga 11 models
(`linear`, `chain`, `residual`, `rms_norm`, `attention`,
`causal_attention`) produce bit-for-bit (or tolerance-bounded)
equivalent outputs on both devices for fixed seeds.

## Phase 3 -- Autograd on MLX (2 steps)

### Step 006 -- tape-lowered ops on MLX + gradcheck parity
`grad(expr, wrt)` works inside a `device("mlx")` block. The
autograd tape's primitives (`add`, `mul`, `matmul`, `exp`,
`log`, `relu`, `tanh`, `sigmoid`, `softmax`, `sum`, `mean`,
`transpose`, `reshape`) all ship MLX-backed backward paths,
each gradcheck-verified against finite differences on fixtures
matching Saga 9's. Where MLX's own `vjp` / `grad` helps,
`mlpl-mlx` is free to use it; where it doesn't, we hand-write
the backward. Either way, the invariant is that
`grad(expr, wrt)` on MLX matches the CPU path to within a
documented tolerance.

### Step 007 -- optimizers + train { } on MLX
`adam`, `momentum_sgd`, `train N { body }`, `last_losses`,
`experiment "name" { }`, and the `_metric` capture all work
unchanged inside `device("mlx") { }`. The `OptimizerState` map
holds MLX-typed tensors when the surrounding scope is MLX.
Parity test: one Adam step on the Saga 11 `tiny_mlp` demo
produces the same parameter update (within tolerance) on CPU
and MLX.

## Phase 4 -- Tiny LM on MLX + benchmarks (1 step)

### Step 008 -- tiny LM MLX variant + bench harness
`demos/tiny_lm_mlx.mlpl` wraps the Saga 13 `demos/tiny_lm.mlpl`
body in `device("mlx") { ... }` and otherwise changes nothing.
Measurable outcome: identical-shape loss curve, >=5x wall-clock
speedup over the interpreter CPU path on an M-class laptop
(target 10-50x per `docs/using-mlx.md`, but 5x is the go / no-go
gate). Extend `mlpl-bench` with an MLX row on the existing
reshape+reduce workload and the Tiny LM training-step workload.
Document the numbers in `docs/benchmarks.md`.

## Phase 5 -- Docs + release (2 steps)

### Step 009 -- tutorial lesson + using-mlx.md retrospective
New web REPL tutorial lesson **"Running on MLX"** that walks
from a `device("cpu") { randn(7, [1024, 64]) }` baseline to the
same expression wrapped in `device("mlx") { ... }`, then shows
a tiny training loop swap. Update `docs/using-mlx.md` from
"design sketch" to "reference" and strip the `> Status: planned`
disclaimer; add a retrospective section summarizing what
actually shipped vs the sketch. Update `docs/status.md`
one-liner and `docs/saga.md` Saga 14 entry. Rebuild `pages/`
via `scripts/build-pages.sh` and commit both source and built
artifact in the same commit.

### Step 010 -- release v0.11.0
Bump workspace version to `0.11.0`. Release commit summarizes
`mlpl-mlx` runtime, `device("...") { }` scoped form,
`to_device(...)` movement helpers, autograd + optimizers on
MLX, and the Tiny LM MLX variant with its measured speedup.
Tag `v0.11.0`. Push commit and tag. Verify the pages workflow
deploys the updated tutorial list.

## Dependencies and risk

- Step 001 depends on `mlx-rs` building cleanly on the dev
  laptop; pin a known-good version in `Cargo.toml`. Risk: MLX
  crate ABI changes between versions. Mitigation: pin minor.
- Step 004 introduces `Expr::Device`. Risk: the scoped form
  interacts with `experiment { }` and `train { }` nesting.
  Mitigation: explicit tests for `experiment { device { train
  { } } }` and the swapped nesting.
- Step 006 is the highest-risk step. MLX's lazy graph and
  MLPL's tape are two autograd systems; we only get to pick
  one per expression. Decide during TDD whether to lean on
  `mlx-rs`'s `vjp` / `grad` wholesale or hand-write backward
  per primitive. Either path must produce the same gradients
  (within tolerance) as the CPU tape.
- Step 008 risk: MLX compile overhead on the first call may
  dominate the Tiny LM's wall clock at this size. Mitigation:
  bench harness reports both cold and warm timings; go/no-go
  is warm-path speedup.
- Cross-platform CI risk: every MLX test must be feature- and
  target-gated so `cargo test` on Linux CI stays green. This
  is an invariant across every step, not a single-step task.

=== PLAN ===
# MLX Backend Milestone (Saga 14, v0.11.0)

## Why this exists

Saga 13 proved the platform thesis on CPU: a few lines of MLPL
train a tiny transformer LM, generate text, and render its own
attention. The next bottleneck is speed. The Saga 13 Tiny LM
trains at interpreter pace on an M-class laptop; an accelerator
backend is the proper lever.

Apple Silicon is this project's baseline dev hardware, and MLX
is its native array framework: lazy graph execution, automatic
operator fusion, unified CPU+GPU memory, and no kernel-per-device
dance. Its array semantics already agree with MLPL's
`DenseArray` (row-major, shape-labeled, consistent broadcast
rules). Saga 14 introduces MLX as MLPL's first accelerator
backend behind the same source surface: a program that trains on
CPU should train on MLX without source changes.

CUDA (Saga 17) is the second accelerator backend. The MLPL
source surface shipped in Saga 14 is the same surface CUDA will
land behind. See `docs/using-mlx.md` for the user-facing design
sketch; this doc is the implementation plan.

## Non-goals

- **No CUDA.** Saga 17.
- **No distributed execution.** Saga 17.
- **No LoRA / quantization.** Saga 15.
- **No new Model DSL layers.** The existing `linear`, `chain`,
  `residual`, `rms_norm`, `attention`, `causal_attention`,
  `embed`, `sinusoidal_encoding`, activations, and
  `cross_entropy` ship on MLX as-is.
- **No checkpoint format.** Still deferred to Saga 15+.
- **No pretraining claims.** Target is the Saga 13 Tiny LM demo
  running faster, not a bigger model.

## Quality requirements (every step)

Identical to Saga 13:

1. TDD: failing test first, then implementation, then refactor.
2. Quality gates must all pass before commit:
   - `cargo test`
   - `cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings`
   - `cargo fmt --all` + `cargo fmt --all -- --check`
   - `markdown-checker -f "**/*.md"` if docs touched
   - `sw-checklist`
3. Use `/mw-cp` checkpoint process.
4. Push immediately after commit.
5. Web UI changes rebuild `pages/` via `scripts/build-pages.sh`.
6. `.agentrail/` changes are committed whenever they change.
7. MLX-feature tests are gated so CI on non-Apple-Silicon hosts
   still passes (`#[cfg(all(target_os = "macos",
   target_arch = "aarch64", feature = "mlx"))]` or the closest
   equivalent supported by `mlx-rs`). Where MLX is unavailable,
   the CPU path remains authoritative.

## What already exists

- `mlpl-rt` runtime target (scalar, array, fncall, variable,
  labels, matmul with static contraction check) used by both
  `mlpl!` proc macro and `mlpl-build` CLI.
- `mlpl-parity-tests` harness proving bit-for-bit agreement
  between interpreter and compile-to-Rust paths on 9 programs.
- `mlpl-bench` Criterion harness comparing interpreter vs
  compiled MLPL (9.05x measured speedup on 100x100 reshape+
  reduce).
- `mlpl-lower-rs` path-configurable AST -> `TokenStream` codegen
  (the `runtime_prefix` knob makes swapping runtimes cheap).
- Full Saga 11/13 Model DSL, autograd, optimizers, and the
  Tiny LM end-to-end demo (`demos/tiny_lm.mlpl`).
- Labeled axes and structured `EvalError::ShapeMismatch`
  propagate through every existing op.

1	//! MLX-backed runtime target for MLPL (Saga 14, Apple Silicon).
2	//!
3	//! Sibling to `mlpl-rt`. Exposes the same primitive surface --
4	//! `matmul`, `add`/`sub`/`mul`/`div`/`neg`, `exp`/`log`/`tanh`/
5	//! `sigmoid`/`relu`, `reshape`/`transpose` -- but dispatches
6	//! through Apple's MLX library via `mlx-rs`. A program compiled
7	//! against `mlpl-rt::<op>` must be swappable for `mlpl_mlx::<op>`
8	//! without source changes; outputs agree bit-for-bit where the
9	//! two runtimes share a dtype, and within a documented fp32
10	//! tolerance where MLX downcasts to f32 for GPU dispatch.
11	//!
12	//! MLX-backed code lives behind three conditions at once:
13	//!
14	//! 1. the `mlx` Cargo feature (opt-in, off by default),
15	//! 2. `target_os = "macos"`,
16	//! 3. `target_arch = "aarch64"`.
17	//!
18	//! On any host that fails any of those, the crate still builds
19	//! but exports no primitives -- the CPU path in `mlpl-rt` remains
20	//! authoritative. The Saga 14 plan treats cross-platform CI as an
21	//! invariant, not a per-step concern; this gating is how we honor
22	//! it.
23	//!
24	//! Phase 1 grows the primitive surface across three steps:
25	//! step 001 introduced `matmul`; step 002 adds the elementwise,
26	//! unary-activation, and shape ops listed above; step 003 will
27	//! add reductions and softmax. Each primitive is in its own
28	//! module so the crate stays under the sw-checklist
29	//! function-count budget as the surface expands.
30	
31	pub use mlpl_array::{ArrayError, DenseArray, Shape};
32	pub use mlpl_core::LabeledShape;
33	
34	#[cfg(all(target_os = "macos", target_arch = "aarch64", feature = "mlx"))]
35	mod activations;
36	#[cfg(all(target_os = "macos", target_arch = "aarch64", feature = "mlx"))]
37	mod common;
38	#[cfg(all(target_os = "macos", target_arch = "aarch64", feature = "mlx"))]
39	mod elementwise;
40	#[cfg(all(target_os = "macos", target_arch = "aarch64", feature = "mlx"))]
41	mod matmul;
42	#[cfg(all(target_os = "macos", target_arch = "aarch64", feature = "mlx"))]
43	mod shapes;
44	
45	#[cfg(all(target_os = "macos", target_arch = "aarch64", feature = "mlx"))]
46	pub use activations::{exp, log, relu, sigmoid, tanh};
47	#[cfg(all(target_os = "macos", target_arch = "aarch64", feature = "mlx"))]
48	pub use elementwise::{add, div, mul, neg, sub};
49	#[cfg(all(target_os = "macos", target_arch = "aarch64", feature = "mlx"))]
50	pub use matmul::matmul;
51	#[cfg(all(target_os = "macos", target_arch = "aarch64", feature = "mlx"))]
52	pub use shapes::{reshape, transpose};
53

1	//! Shape and reduction primitives (Saga 14 step 002 refactor of lib.rs).
2	//!
3	//! Kept in its own module so `lib.rs` stays under the sw-checklist
4	//! function-count budget while Saga 14 grows the runtime surface.
5	
6	use mlpl_array::{ArrayError, DenseArray, Shape};
7	
8	/// `reshape(a, dims)` -- reinterpret `a` with new dims. Element
9	/// count must match or `ArrayError::ShapeMismatch` is returned.
10	/// Labels drop (Saga 11.5 semantics -- a reshaped array no longer
11	/// carries the old per-axis identity).
12	pub fn reshape(a: &DenseArray, dims: &[usize]) -> Result<DenseArray, ArrayError> {
13	    a.reshape(Shape::new(dims.to_vec()))
14	}
15	
16	/// `transpose(a)` -- reverse axis order. Preserves Saga 11.5 labels
17	/// (labels reverse alongside axes).
18	#[must_use]
19	pub fn transpose(a: &DenseArray) -> DenseArray {
20	    a.transpose()
21	}
22	
23	/// `reduce_add(a)` -- flat sum of all elements as a scalar.
24	#[must_use]
25	pub fn reduce_add(a: &DenseArray) -> DenseArray {
26	    let sum: f64 = a.data().iter().copied().sum();
27	    DenseArray::from_scalar(sum)
28	}
29	
30	/// `reduce_add_axis(a, axis)` -- sum along `axis`, dropping that
31	/// dim (and its label, if any) from the result shape.
32	pub fn reduce_add_axis(a: &DenseArray, axis: usize) -> Result<DenseArray, ArrayError> {
33	    a.reduce_axis(axis, 0.0, |x, y| x + y)
34	}
35

1	//! MLX-backed shape primitives (Saga 14 step 002).
2	//!
3	//! Shape-metadata ops -- `reshape` and `transpose` -- dispatch
4	//! through MLX's own shape kernels so the Saga 14 promise "every
5	//! primitive runs on MLX" holds end-to-end. Values travel through
6	//! the fp32 round trip in `common::dense_to_mlx` + `mlx_to_dense_data`
7	//! like every other op, so parity tests share the same tolerance
8	//! budget.
9	//!
10	//! Label propagation mirrors `mlpl-array`:
11	//! - `reshape` drops labels (a reshaped array no longer carries
12	//!   the old per-axis identity).
13	//! - `transpose` reverses the label list alongside the axes.
14	
15	use mlpl_array::{ArrayError, DenseArray, Shape};
16	
17	use crate::common::{dense_to_mlx, finalize, mlx_to_dense_data};
18	
19	/// `reshape(a, dims)` -- reinterpret `a` with new dims. Element
20	/// count must match or `ArrayError::ShapeMismatch` is returned.
21	/// Labels drop, matching the CPU path.
22	pub fn reshape(a: &DenseArray, dims: &[usize]) -> Result<DenseArray, ArrayError> {
23	    let source = a.elem_count();
24	    let target: usize = dims.iter().product();
25	    if source != target {
26	        return Err(ArrayError::ShapeMismatch { source, target });
27	    }
28	    let mlx = dense_to_mlx(a.data(), a.shape().dims());
29	    let new_shape_i32: Vec<i32> = dims.iter().map(|&d| d as i32).collect();
30	    let reshaped = mlx
31	        .reshape(&new_shape_i32)
32	        .expect("mlx reshape on validated element count");
33	    finalize(Shape::new(dims.to_vec()), mlx_to_dense_data(reshaped), None)
34	}
35	
36	/// `transpose(a)` -- reverse axis order and reorder data to
37	/// row-major. Labels reverse alongside axes. Infallible, matching
38	/// `mlpl_rt::transpose`.
39	///
40	/// Implementation note: MLX 0.25.3 exposes `transpose` as a lazy
41	/// view with permuted strides, and `as_slice` on a view still
42	/// returns the underlying row-major buffer -- mlx-rs does not
43	/// surface the C `mlx_contiguous` helper that would force a
44	/// rematerialized copy. We therefore delegate to the CPU path,
45	/// which is a fixed-size stride walk and cheap at Saga 14 sizes.
46	/// Once `device("mlx") { }` chains fuse transpose with downstream
47	/// ops (Phase 2, step 005), the intermediate view never lands in
48	/// a flat buffer and this fallback disappears.
49	#[must_use]
50	pub fn transpose(a: &DenseArray) -> DenseArray {
51	    a.transpose()
52	}
53

Author: Mike Wright <mike@softwarewrighter.com>

    feat(mlpl-mlx): Saga 14 step 002 -- elementwise + shape ops on MLX
    
    Fill out the forward-pass primitive surface so the MLX runtime can
    compute every non-reduction op the Tiny LM uses. Each primitive
    mirrors the corresponding mlpl-rt signature, so codegen can swap
    runtimes without source changes.
    
    New primitives in mlpl-mlx (all triple-gated behind target_os=macos
    + target_arch=aarch64 + feature=mlx):
    
      elementwise: add, sub, mul, div, neg
      activations: exp, log, tanh, sigmoid, relu
      shape:       reshape, transpose
    
    Binary ops validate shapes (same-shape or scalar-broadcast) on the
    Rust side before dispatch, merge labels via the mirror of mlpl-
    array's merge_labels, and route through MLX (fp32) via shared
    convert helpers. Unary ops are infallible -- output shape and
    labels come from the input so no intermediate can fail.
    
    transpose falls back to the CPU DenseArray implementation because
    mlx-rs 0.25.3 exposes transpose as a lazy view with permuted
    strides and does not expose mlx_contiguous for forced
    rematerialization; as_slice on a view returns the original row-
    major buffer. Phase 2's device("mlx") { } chains will make this
    a non-issue (the view composes downstream without ever landing
    in a flat buffer).
    
    Also add the same primitive signatures to mlpl-rt (infallible
    activations, Result<_> binary ops) as thin wrappers over
    DenseArray helpers. Both crates split their surface across
    elementwise/activations/shapes modules to stay under the
    sw-checklist function-count budget.

1	//! Elementwise binary primitives (Saga 14 step 002).
2	//!
3	//! Thin wrappers over `DenseArray::apply_binop` that let compiled
4	//! MLPL code call `mlpl_rt::add(&a, &b)` instead of a trait method.
5	//! The `mlpl-mlx` sibling crate exposes the same signatures so the
6	//! compile-to-rust codegen can target either runtime without source
7	//! changes.
8	//!
9	//! Label propagation is inherited from `apply_binop` -- the
10	//! non-scalar side's labels win, or matching label vectors
11	//! pass through, or `LabelMismatch` is raised.
12	
13	use mlpl_array::{ArrayError, DenseArray};
14	
15	/// Elementwise `a + b` with scalar broadcasting.
16	pub fn add(a: &DenseArray, b: &DenseArray) -> Result<DenseArray, ArrayError> {
17	    a.apply_binop(b, |x, y| x + y)
18	}
19	
20	/// Elementwise `a - b` with scalar broadcasting.
21	pub fn sub(a: &DenseArray, b: &DenseArray) -> Result<DenseArray, ArrayError> {
22	    a.apply_binop(b, |x, y| x - y)
23	}
24	
25	/// Elementwise `a * b` (Hadamard product) with scalar broadcasting.
26	pub fn mul(a: &DenseArray, b: &DenseArray) -> Result<DenseArray, ArrayError> {
27	    a.apply_binop(b, |x, y| x * y)
28	}
29	
30	/// Elementwise `a / b` with scalar broadcasting.
31	pub fn div(a: &DenseArray, b: &DenseArray) -> Result<DenseArray, ArrayError> {
32	    a.apply_binop(b, |x, y| x / y)
33	}
34	
35	/// Unary elementwise negation. Labels propagate unchanged.
36	#[must_use]
37	pub fn neg(a: &DenseArray) -> DenseArray {
38	    a.map(|x| -x)
39	}

15	/// Applies the element-wise sigmoid logistic sigmoid.
16	///
17	/// For details, please see
18	/// [this documentation](https://ml-explore.github.io/mlx/build/html/python/_autosummary/mlx.core.sigmoid.html)
19	///
20	/// This is:
21	///
22	/// ```rust, ignore
23	/// sigmoid(x)
24	/// ```
25	pub fn sigmoid(x: impl AsRef<Array>) -> Result<Array> {
26	    crate::ops::sigmoid(x.as_ref())
27	}
28	
29	/// Applies the Rectified Linear Unit.
30	///
31	/// This is:
32	///
33	/// ```rust, ignore
34	/// maximum(x, 0)
35	/// ```
36	pub fn relu(x: impl AsRef<Array>) -> Result<Array> {
37	    crate::ops::maximum(x.as_ref(), &array!(0))
38	}
39	
40	/// Applies the Leaky Rectified Linear Unit.
41	///
42	/// `neg_slope` is default to 0.01 if not provided.
43	///
44	/// This is:
45	///
46	/// ```rust, ignore
47	/// maximum(neg_slope * x, x)
48	/// ```

1	//! Error types for mlpl-array.
2	
3	/// Errors produced by array operations.
4	#[derive(Clone, Debug, PartialEq, Eq)]
5	pub enum ArrayError {
6	    /// Data length does not match shape element count.
7	    DataLengthMismatch {
8	        /// Expected length (from shape).
9	        expected: usize,
10	        /// Actual data length provided.
11	        got: usize,
12	    },
13	    /// Reshape target element count differs from source.
14	    ShapeMismatch {
15	        /// Source element count.
16	        source: usize,
17	        /// Target element count.
18	        target: usize,
19	    },
20	    /// Index component is out of bounds for its axis.
21	    IndexOutOfBounds {
22	        /// Which axis.
23	        axis: usize,
24	        /// The index that was given.
25	        index: usize,
26	        /// The size of that axis.
27	        size: usize,
28	    },
29	    /// Index has wrong number of components for the array rank.
30	    RankMismatch {
31	        /// Expected rank.
32	        expected: usize,
33	        /// Number of index components given.
34	        got: usize,
35	    },
36	    /// Attempted to index an empty array.
37	    EmptyArray,
38	    /// Label list does not match array rank (Saga 11.5 Phase 2).
39	    LabelsRankMismatch {
40	        /// Array rank (number of axes).
41	        rank: usize,
42	        /// Number of labels the caller provided.
43	        labels: usize,
44	    },
45	    /// Two labeled operands disagree on axis labels (Saga 11.5 Phase 3).
46	    ///
47	    /// Raised by `apply_binop` when both sides carry labels but the
48	    /// label vectors differ. Step 006 will lift this into a
49	    /// richer `EvalError::ShapeMismatch` at the evaluator boundary.
50	    LabelMismatch {
51	        /// Labels on the first (left-hand) operand.
52	        expected: Vec<Option<String>>,
53	        /// Labels on the second (right-hand) operand.
54	        actual: Vec<Option<String>>,
55	    },
56	}
57	
58	impl std::fmt::Display for ArrayError {
59	    fn fmt(&self, f: &mut std::fmt::Formatter<'_>) -> std::fmt::Result {
60	        match self {
61	            Self::DataLengthMismatch { expected, got } => {
62	                write!(
63	                    f,
64	                    "data length {got} does not match shape element count {expected}"
65	                )
66	            }
67	            Self::ShapeMismatch { source, target } => {
68	                write!(f, "shape mismatch: {source} vs {target} elements")
69	            }
70	            Self::IndexOutOfBounds { axis, index, size } => {
71	                write!(
72	                    f,
73	                    "index {index} out of bounds for axis {axis} with size {size}"
74	                )
75	            }
76	            Self::RankMismatch { expected, got } => {
77	                write!(
78	                    f,
79	                    "index has {got} components but array has rank {expected}"
80	                )
81	            }
82	            Self::EmptyArray => write!(f, "cannot index an empty array"),
83	            Self::LabelsRankMismatch { rank, labels } => {
84	                write!(
85	                    f,
86	                    "label list has {labels} entries but array has rank {rank}"
87	                )
88	            }
89	            Self::LabelMismatch { expected, actual } => {
90	                write!(
91	                    f,
92	                    "axis label mismatch: expected {expected:?}, got {actual:?}"
93	                )
94	            }
95	        }
96	    }
97	}

file.content:
[package]
name = "mlpl-mlx"
edition.workspace = true
license.workspace = true
version.workspace = true

[features]
default = []
# Apple Silicon only. The `cfg(all(target_os = "macos", target_arch =
# "aarch64"))` guard inside the crate keeps the feature compilable on
# non-Apple hosts (where `mlx-rs`' C++/Metal toolchain would fail to
# build), making `cargo test -p mlpl-mlx --features mlx` safe to run
# from a Linux CI host: it stubs out to a no-op surface.
mlx = ["dep:mlx-rs"]

[dependencies]
mlpl-array = { path = "../mlpl-array" }
mlpl-core = { path = "../mlpl-core" }

# Pinned by Saga 14 plan (risk/mitigation item): upstream ABI can
# shift between minor versions. Bump deliberately, not transitively.
#
# `default-features = false` drops the `metal` feature so mlx-sys
# does not require the `metal` shader compiler (ships only with a
# full Xcode.app, not the Command Line Tools). We keep
# `accelerate` on -- that's Apple's CPU/SIMD BLAS, which is all
# Saga 14 step 001 needs to prove the crate integrates. A later
# step re-enables `metal` behind its own cfg gate once the Xcode
# dependency is documented in the project bootstrap.
mlx-rs = { version = "=0.25.3", optional = true, default-features = false, features = [
  "accelerate",
] }

[dev-dependencies]
# Saga 14 step 002: parity tests compare MLX outputs against the
# CPU path in `mlpl-rt`. Dev-only so `mlpl-mlx` itself stays
# decoupled from the CPU runtime crate at link time.
mlpl-rt = { path = "../mlpl-rt" }

# Tiny Language Model -- end-to-end training on a Shakespeare snippet.
#
# Saga 13 step 006 demo: wires every Saga 13 primitive together with
# the existing BPE tokenizer, dataset surface, and experiment tracking.
# All on CPU, reproducible via fixed seeds.

# ---- corpus + tokenization ----
corpus = load_preloaded("tiny_shakespeare_snippet")
tok    = train_bpe(corpus, 280, 0)
ids    = apply_tokenizer(tok, corpus)

# ---- shift-pair windows: input tokens and next-token labels ----
X_all = shift_pairs_x(ids, 32)
Y_all = shift_pairs_y(ids, 32)
X     = reshape(X_all, [reduce_mul(shape(X_all))])
Y     = reshape(Y_all, [reduce_mul(shape(Y_all))])

# ---- model: embed -> positional -> 1-block transformer -> project ----
V = 280 ; d = 32 ; h = 1
model = chain(embed(V, d, 0), residual(chain(rms_norm(d), causal_attention(d, h, 1))), residual(chain(rms_norm(d), linear(d, 128, 2), relu_layer(), linear(128, d, 3))), rms_norm(d), linear(d, V, 4))

# ---- training ----
experiment "tiny_lm" {
  train 200 {
    adam(cross_entropy(apply(model, X), Y), model, 0.001, 0.9, 0.999, 0.00000001);
    loss_metric = cross_entropy(apply(model, X), Y)
  }
}
loss_curve(last_losses)

# Tiny Language Model -- end-to-end training on a Shakespeare snippet.
#
# Saga 13 step 006 demo: wires every Saga 13 primitive together with
# the existing BPE tokenizer, dataset surface, and experiment tracking.
# All on CPU, reproducible via fixed seeds.

# ---- corpus + tokenization ----
corpus = load_preloaded("tiny_shakespeare_snippet")
tok    = train_bpe(corpus, 280, 0)
ids    = apply_tokenizer(tok, corpus)

# ---- shift-pair windows: input tokens and next-token labels ----
X_all = shift_pairs_x(ids, 32)
Y_all = shift_pairs_y(ids, 32)
X     = reshape(X_all, [reduce_mul(shape(X_all))])
Y     = reshape(Y_all, [reduce_mul(shape(Y_all))])

# ---- model: embed -> positional -> 1-block transformer -> project ----
V = 280 ; d = 32 ; h = 1
model = chain(embed(V, d, 0), residual(chain(rms_norm(d), causal_attention(d, h, 1))), residual(chain(rms_norm(d), linear(d, 128, 2), relu_layer(), linear(128, d, 3))), rms_norm(d), linear(d, V, 4))

# ---- training ----
experiment "tiny_lm" {
  train 200 {
    adam(cross_entropy(apply(model, X), Y), model, 0.001, 0.9, 0.999, 0.00000001);
    loss_metric = cross_entropy(apply(model, X), Y)
  }
}
loss_curve(last_losses)

89	### Step 002 -- core elementwise + shape ops on MLX
90	Port the shape- and element-level primitives the existing
91	interpreter uses most: `add`/`sub`/`mul`/`div`/`neg`, `exp`/
92	`log`/`tanh`/`sigmoid`/`relu`, `reshape`, `transpose`. Each
93	primitive lands with a unit test + parity test vs `mlpl-rt`.
94	Labels propagate identically -- the MLX runtime does not own
95	`LabeledShape`, it borrows `mlpl-core`'s. Gradcheck is not in
96	scope yet (step 006).
97	
98	### Step 003 -- reductions + softmax + log-softmax on MLX
99	`reduce_add`/`reduce_mul`/`mean`/`argmax` (axis-aware, label-
100	aware), `softmax`, `log_softmax`, and `cross_entropy`.
101	Numerical-stability invariants from the CPU path (max-
102	subtraction log-softmax) carry over. Parity tests on `[4, 3]`
103	and `[2, 3, 5]` fixtures. At the end of this phase the MLX
104	runtime can compute forward passes of every primitive the Tiny
105	LM uses.

--features mlx` gates the test; default build stays green on
non-Apple hosts.

### Step 002 -- core elementwise + shape ops on MLX
Port the shape- and element-level primitives the existing
interpreter uses most: `add`/`sub`/`mul`/`div`/`neg`, `exp`/
`log`/`tanh`/`sigmoid`/`relu`, `reshape`, `transpose`. Each
primitive lands with a unit test + parity test vs `mlpl-rt`.
Labels propagate identically -- the MLX runtime does not own
`LabeledShape`, it borrows `mlpl-core`'s. Gradcheck is not in
scope yet (step 006).

### Step 003 -- reductions + softmax + log-softmax on MLX
`reduce_add`/`reduce_mul`/`mean`/`argmax` (axis-aware, label-
aware), `softmax`, `log_softmax`, and `cross_entropy`.
Numerical-stability invariants from the CPU path (max-
subtraction log-softmax) carry over. Parity tests on `[4, 3]`
and `[2, 3, 5]` fixtures. At the end of this phase the MLX
runtime can compute forward passes of every primitive the Tiny
LM uses.

# Saga

## Saga -1: Repo scaffolding (COMPLETE)
Repo compartmentalization scaffolding.

## Saga 0: Foundation (COMPLETE)
Foundation and contracts.

## Saga 1: Dense tensor substrate v1 (COMPLETE)
Shape, DenseArray, reshape, transpose, indexing.

## Saga 2: Parser and evaluator foundation (COMPLETE)
Lexer, AST, parser with precedence, AST-walking evaluator.

## Saga 3: CLI and REPL v1 (COMPLETE)
Working REPL with all v1 syntax, built-in functions, tracing.

## Saga 4: Structured trace v1 (COMPLETE)
TraceEvent/Trace types, evaluator instrumentation, JSON export.

## Saga 5: Visual web viewer v1 (DEFERRED)
Deferred to post-MVP. MVP uses CLI + JSON trace export.

## MVP
Sagas 0 through 4 complete. MVP ships with REPL + trace export.

## Saga 6: ML foundations (COMPLETE)
Sigmoid, tanh_fn, pow, comparison operators, axis reductions, mean,
array constructors (zeros, ones, fill), and the first end-to-end
logistic regression demo. Delivered v0.2.

## Saga 7: SVG visualization v1 (COMPLETE)
`mlpl-viz` crate, `svg(data, type[, aux])` built-in, diagram types
(scatter, line, bar, heatmap, decision_boundary), `grid()` helper,
high-level analysis helpers (hist, scatter_labeled, loss_curve,
confusion_matrix, boundary_2d), browser REPL inline SVG rendering,
and download button. Delivered v0.3.

## Saga 11: Model DSL (COMPLETE)
Composition primitives for neural-net models: a new `Value::Model`

160	`sinusoidal_encoding(T, d_model)` additive positional tables;
161	`causal_attention(d_model, heads, seed)` with a lower-triangular
162	pre-softmax mask so position `t` cannot peek at `t+1`;
163	numerically-stable `cross_entropy(logits, targets)` over integer
164	targets with max-subtraction log-softmax; `sample(logits, t,
165	seed)` multinomial draws plus `top_k(logits, k)` pre-softmax
166	restriction; and a `last_row` / `concat` / `attention_weights`
167	triple that makes a generation loop and a `[T, T]` attention
168	heatmap one-liner. New demos `demos/tiny_lm.mlpl` (end-to-end
169	training) and `demos/tiny_lm_generate.mlpl` (generation +
170	heatmap) wire every Saga 13 primitive together with Saga 12's
171	BPE tokenizer, `shift_pairs_x`/`shift_pairs_y` next-token pair
172	windows, and `experiment "name" { }`-tracked `train N { adam(... )
173	}`. Two new web REPL tutorial lessons -- "Language Model Basics"
174	(forward-only, runs in <2s) and "Training and Generating"
175	(stripped-down training loop + 20-token generation + attention
176	heatmap) -- ship alongside. Delivered v0.10.0. See

179	## Saga 12: Tokenizers, datasets, and experiment tracking (COMPLETE)
180	Closes the last surface-only gap before the Tiny LM. File IO:
181	`load("rel.csv")` / `load("rel.txt")` reads through an
182	`Environment::data_dir` sandbox (terminal REPL `--data-dir`
183	flag); `load_preloaded("name")` serves compiled-in corpora for
184	the web REPL. Dataset prep: `shuffle(x, seed)` Fisher-Yates row
185	permutation, `batch(x, size)` with zero-padded short batches +
186	matching `batch_mask`, `split(x, frac, seed)` and
187	`val_split(x, frac, seed)` returning disjoint chunks; `for row
188	in ds { body }` streaming iteration with `last_rows` capture
189	(new `Token::For`/`Token::In` keywords, `Expr::For` AST
190	variant). Byte-level BPE: `tokenize_bytes` / `decode_bytes`
191	primitives; `Value::Tokenizer` runtime variant with
192	`TokenizerSpec::ByteLevel` and `TokenizerSpec::BpeMerges`;
193	`train_bpe(corpus, vocab_size, seed)` with deterministic
194	lex-smallest tie-breaking; `apply_tokenizer(tok, text)` +
195	`decode(tok, tokens)` byte-lossless round-trip for any UTF-8
196	input. Experiment tracking: `experiment "name" { body }` scoped
197	form captures `_metric`-suffixed scalars and bound params'
198	shapes into `ExperimentRecord`s, logged to

`sinusoidal_encoding(T, d_model)` additive positional tables;
`causal_attention(d_model, heads, seed)` with a lower-triangular
pre-softmax mask so position `t` cannot peek at `t+1`;
numerically-stable `cross_entropy(logits, targets)` over integer
targets with max-subtraction log-softmax; `sample(logits, t,
seed)` multinomial draws plus `top_k(logits, k)` pre-softmax
restriction; and a `last_row` / `concat` / `attention_weights`
triple that makes a generation loop and a `[T, T]` attention
heatmap one-liner. New demos `demos/tiny_lm.mlpl` (end-to-end
training) and `demos/tiny_lm_generate.mlpl` (generation +
heatmap) wire every Saga 13 primitive together with Saga 12's
BPE tokenizer, `shift_pairs_x`/`shift_pairs_y` next-token pair
windows, and `experiment "name" { }`-tracked `train N { adam(... )
}`. Two new web REPL tutorial lessons -- "Language Model Basics"
(forward-only, runs in <2s) and "Training and Generating"
(stripped-down training loop + 20-token generation + attention
heatmap) -- ship alongside. Delivered v0.10.0. See
`docs/milestone-tiny-lm.md`.

## Saga 12: Tokenizers, datasets, and experiment tracking (COMPLETE)
Closes the last surface-only gap before the Tiny LM. File IO:
`load("rel.csv")` / `load("rel.txt")` reads through an
`Environment::data_dir` sandbox (terminal REPL `--data-dir`
flag); `load_preloaded("name")` serves compiled-in corpora for
the web REPL. Dataset prep: `shuffle(x, seed)` Fisher-Yates row
permutation, `batch(x, size)` with zero-padded short batches +
matching `batch_mask`, `split(x, frac, seed)` and
`val_split(x, frac, seed)` returning disjoint chunks; `for row
in ds { body }` streaming iteration with `last_rows` capture
(new `Token::For`/`Token::In` keywords, `Expr::For` AST
variant). Byte-level BPE: `tokenize_bytes` / `decode_bytes`
primitives; `Value::Tokenizer` runtime variant with
`TokenizerSpec::ByteLevel` and `TokenizerSpec::BpeMerges`;
`train_bpe(corpus, vocab_size, seed)` with deterministic
lex-smallest tie-breaking; `apply_tokenizer(tok, text)` +
`decode(tok, tokens)` byte-lossless round-trip for any UTF-8
input. Experiment tracking: `experiment "name" { body }` scoped
form captures `_metric`-suffixed scalars and bound params'
shapes into `ExperimentRecord`s, logged to
`env.experiment_log` and (terminal REPL `--exp-dir`)

content.content: Saga 14 step 002 shipped. mlpl-mlx now exposes forward-pass primitives for add/sub/mul/div/neg, exp/log/tanh/sigmoid/relu, reshape/transpose, each cfg-gated behind target_os=macos + aarch64 + feature=mlx and mirrored by infallible/Result-typed signatures on mlpl-rt for codegen parity. Binary ops validate shapes on the Rust side, merge labels via a local mirror of mlpl-array::merge_labels, then dispatch through shared fp32 convert helpers in common.rs. transpose falls back to DenseArray::transpose because mlx-rs 0.25.3 exposes transpose as a lazy strided view and hides mlx_contiguous, so as_slice on a view still returns the original row-major buffer. Both crates split across elementwise/activations/shapes/transforms modules to stay under the sw-checklist function-count budget. 17 MLX parity tests pass; default build + non-MLX cargo test stay green on non-Apple hosts.

Binary ops validate shapes on the Rust side, merge labels via a local mirror of mlpl-array::merge_labels, then dispatch through shared fp32 convert helpers in common.rs. transpose falls back to DenseArray::transpose because mlx-rs 0.25.3 exposes transpose as a lazy strided view and hides mlx_contiguous, so as_slice on a view still returns the original row-major buffer. Both crates split across elementwise/activations/shapes/transforms modules to stay under the sw-checklist function-count budget. 17 MLX parity tests pass; default build + non-MLX cargo test stay green on non-Apple hosts.

input.command:
git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
feat(mlpl-mlx): Saga 14 step 003 -- reductions + softmax + CE on MLX

Close the forward-pass gap so the Tiny LM's forward can run on the
MLX runtime end-to-end. Each new primitive mirrors a sibling
signature on `mlpl-rt`, so compiled MLPL code can swap runtimes
without source changes.

New primitives in `mlpl-mlx` (and matching wrappers in `mlpl-rt`),
all triple-gated behind target_os=macos + target_arch=aarch64 +
feature=mlx on the MLX side:

  reductions: reduce_mul, mean, argmax (Option<usize> axis)
  normalisation: softmax, log_softmax (usize axis)
  loss: cross_entropy(logits, targets)

Reductions are axis-aware and label-aware: a reduced axis drops
its label (Saga 11.5), softmax/log_softmax preserve labels
(shape-preserving op), cross_entropy returns an unlabeled scalar
mean. Validation (axis bounds, target integrality, target range)
lives on the Rust side so MLX sees only legal inputs; numerical
work routes through mlx-rs's own kernels (prod/mean/argmax,
softmax_axis, nn::log_softmax, logsumexp_axis). The
cross_entropy implementation runs the per-row LSE through MLX
once and does the gather + mean on the Rust side -- two fp32
round-trips total, so the parity tolerance is widened to 2 *
FP32_TOL on that op.

Both crates land a new `reductions.rs` module to host the new
surface; mlpl-rt and mlpl-mlx are now at the sw-checklist 7-
modules-per-crate ceiling. CPU-side semantics are pinned by
`mlpl-rt`'s primitives_tests.rs so the MLX parity tests have a
trusted gold standard without dragging in `mlpl-runtime` as a
dev-dep.

Parity coverage on the prompt-specified fixtures:

  reductions + softmax + log_softmax: [4, 3] and [2, 3, 5]
  cross_entropy: [B*T = 8, V = 5] with mixed-sign logits

Closing test in `tiny_lm_forward_tests.rs` runs a structurally-
identical Tiny LM forward (embed -> positional -> rms_norm ->
causal_attention -> residual -> rms_norm -> linear -> relu ->
linear -> residual -> rms_norm -> linear -> cross_entropy) on
both runtimes via a function-pointer Backend table and asserts
the loss agrees within 5e-4 (the per-element fp32 error compounds
roughly linearly through ~10 chained MLX kernels). The model is
scaled down for test speed (V=8, d=4, T=4, ff=8) but uses the
same primitives and shape contracts as `demos/tiny_lm.mlpl`.

Quality gates green: 28 mlpl-mlx tests pass under --features
mlx, default `cargo test -p mlpl-mlx` still passes 0 tests on
non-Apple-Silicon paths (cross-platform CI invariant), workspace
`cargo test` clean, clippy --all-targets --all-features clean,
fmt clean, sw-checklist adds no new FAILs (only WARN-level
function-LOC and module-count notices, all under their hard
ceilings).

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)"

content.content:
=== PLAN ===
# MLX Backend Milestone (Saga 14, v0.11.0)

## Why this exists

Saga 13 proved the platform thesis on CPU: a few lines of MLPL
train a tiny transformer LM, generate text, and render its own
attention. The next bottleneck is speed. The Saga 13 Tiny LM
trains at interpreter pace on an M-class laptop; an accelerator
backend is the proper lever.

Apple Silicon is this project's baseline dev hardware, and MLX
is its native array framework: lazy graph execution, automatic
operator fusion, unified CPU+GPU memory, and no kernel-per-device
dance. Its array semantics already agree with MLPL's
`DenseArray` (row-major, shape-labeled, consistent broadcast
rules). Saga 14 introduces MLX as MLPL's first accelerator
backend behind the same source surface: a program that trains on
CPU should train on MLX without source changes.

CUDA (Saga 17) is the second accelerator backend. The MLPL
source surface shipped in Saga 14 is the same surface CUDA will
land behind. See `docs/using-mlx.md` for the user-facing design
sketch; this doc is the implementation plan.

## Non-goals

- **No CUDA.** Saga 17.
- **No distributed execution.** Saga 17.
- **No LoRA / quantization.** Saga 15.
- **No new Model DSL layers.** The existing `linear`, `chain`,
  `residual`, `rms_norm`, `attention`, `causal_attention`,
  `embed`, `sinusoidal_encoding`, activations, and
  `cross_entropy` ship on MLX as-is.
- **No checkpoint format.** Still deferred to Saga 15+.
- **No pretraining claims.** Target is the Saga 13 Tiny LM demo
  running faster, not a bigger model.

## Quality requirements (every step)

Identical to Saga 13:

1. TDD: failing test first, then implementation, then refactor.
2. Quality gates must all pass before commit:
   - `cargo test`
   - `cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings`
   - `cargo fmt --all` + `cargo fmt --all -- --check`
   - `markdown-checker -f "**/*.md"` if docs touched
   - `sw-checklist`
3. Use `/mw-cp` checkpoint process.
4. Push immediately after commit.
5. Web UI changes rebuild `pages/` via `scripts/build-pages.sh`.
6. `.agentrail/` changes are committed whenever they change.
7. MLX-feature tests are gated so CI on non-Apple-Silicon hosts
   still passes (`#[cfg(all(target_os = "macos",
   target_arch = "aarch64", feature = "mlx"))]` or the closest
   equivalent supported by `mlx-rs`). Where MLX is unavailable,
   the CPU path remains authoritative.

## What already exists

- `mlpl-rt` runtime target (scalar, array, fncall, variable,
  labels, matmul with static contraction check) used by both
  `mlpl!` proc macro and `mlpl-build` CLI.
- `mlpl-parity-tests` harness proving bit-for-bit agreement
  between interpreter and compile-to-Rust paths on 9 programs.
- `mlpl-bench` Criterion harness comparing interpreter vs
  compiled MLPL (9.05x measured speedup on 100x100 reshape+
  reduce).
- `mlpl-lower-rs` path-configurable AST -> `TokenStream` codegen
  (the `runtime_prefix` knob makes swapping runtimes cheap).
- Full Saga 11/13 Model DSL, autograd, optimizers, and the
  Tiny LM end-to-end demo (`demos/tiny_lm.mlpl`).
- Labeled axes and structured `EvalError::ShapeMismatch`
  propagate through every existing op.

## Phase 1 -- mlpl-mlx runtime target (3 steps)

### Step 001 -- mlpl-mlx crate skeleton + first primitive
New `crates/mlpl-mlx` sibling to `mlpl-rt`. `mlx-rs` dependency
behind a `mlx` Cargo feature. Ports exactly one primitive --
`matmul(a, b)` -- backed by MLX's `matmul` and returning an
MLX array wrapped in the same `DenseArray` / `LabeledShape`
shape as `mlpl-rt`. Parity test vs `mlpl-rt::matmul` on a
`[8, 4] @ [4, 8]` fixture (bit-for-bit or within a documented
fp32 tolerance; decide during TDD). `cargo test -p mlpl-mlx
--features mlx` gates the test; default build stays green on
non-Apple hosts.

### Step 002 -- core elementwise + shape ops on MLX
Port the shape- and element-level primitives the existing
interpreter uses most: `add`/`sub`/`mul`/`div`/`neg`, `exp`/
`log`/`tanh`/`sigmoid`/`relu`, `reshape`, `transpose`. Each
primitive lands with a unit test + parity test vs `mlpl-rt`.
Labels propagate identically -- the MLX runtime does not own
`LabeledShape`, it borrows `mlpl-core`'s. Gradcheck is not in
scope yet (step 006).

### Step 003 -- reductions + softmax + log-softmax on MLX
`reduce_add`/`reduce_mul`/`mean`/`argmax` (axis-aware, label-
aware), `softmax`, `log_softmax`, and `cross_entropy`.
Numerical-stability invariants from the CPU path (max-
subtraction log-softmax) carry over. Parity tests on `[4, 3]`
and `[2, 3, 5]` fixtures. At the end of this phase the MLX
runtime can compute forward passes of every primitive the Tiny
LM uses.

## Phase 2 -- Device placement + backend dispatch (2 steps)

### Step 004 -- device("...") scoped form + parser surface
New `device("mlx") { body }` scoped form in the parser/AST
(`Expr::Device`), mirroring `experiment`'s shape. Inside the
block, evaluator dispatches array allocations and ops through
the MLX runtime when the `mlx` feature is enabled; falls back
to CPU (with a one-time warning) otherwise. Labels and shapes
propagate identically across the boundary. Tests: parser
round-trip, evaluator returns the same shape+labels as the CPU
path, `device("cpu") { }` is a no-op and works on every host.

### Step 005 -- backend dispatch in the Model DSL
`apply(model, X)` and `params(model)` route through the active
backend when `X` was allocated inside a `device("mlx")` block.
A model owns its parameters on one device -- moving between
devices requires an explicit `to_device("cpu")` / `to_device
("mlx")` builtin (single-direction helpers that copy tensors;
tests cover round-trip equality). The Saga 11 models
(`linear`, `chain`, `residual`, `rms_norm`, `attention`,
`causal_attention`) produce bit-for-bit (or tolerance-bounded)
equivalent outputs on both devices for fixed seeds.

## Phase 3 -- Autograd on MLX (2 steps)

### Step 006 -- tape-lowered ops on MLX + gradcheck parity
`grad(expr, wrt)` works inside a `device("mlx")` block. The
autograd tape's primitives (`add`, `mul`, `matmul`, `exp`,
`log`, `relu`, `tanh`, `sigmoid`, `softmax`, `sum`, `mean`,
`transpose`, `reshape`) all ship MLX-backed backward paths,
each gradcheck-verified against finite differences on fixtures
matching Saga 9's. Where MLX's own `vjp` / `grad` helps,
`mlpl-mlx` is free to use it; where it doesn't, we hand-write
the backward. Either way, the invariant is that
`grad(expr, wrt)` on MLX matches the CPU path to within a
documented tolerance.

### Step 007 -- optimizers + train { } on MLX
`adam`, `momentum_sgd`, `train N { body }`, `last_losses`,
`experiment "name" { }`, and the `_metric` capture all work
unchanged inside `device("mlx") { }`. The `OptimizerState` map
holds MLX-typed tensors when the surrounding scope is MLX.
Parity test: one Adam step on the Saga 11 `tiny_mlp` demo
produces the same parameter update (within tolerance) on CPU
and MLX.

## Phase 4 -- Tiny LM on MLX + benchmarks (1 step)

### Step 008 -- tiny LM MLX variant + bench harness
`demos/tiny_lm_mlx.mlpl` wraps the Saga 13 `demos/tiny_lm.mlpl`
body in `device("mlx") { ... }` and otherwise changes nothing.
Measurable outcome: identical-shape loss curve, >=5x wall-clock
speedup over the interpreter CPU path on an M-class laptop
(target 10-50x per `docs/using-mlx.md`, but 5x is the go / no-go
gate). Extend `mlpl-bench` with an MLX row on the existing
reshape+reduce workload and the Tiny LM training-step workload.
Document the numbers in `docs/benchmarks.md`.

## Phase 5 -- Docs + release (2 steps)

### Step 009 -- tutorial lesson + using-mlx.md retrospective
New web REPL tutorial lesson **"Running on MLX"** that walks
from a `device("cpu") { randn(7, [1024, 64]) }` baseline to the
same expression wrapped in `device("mlx") { ... }`, then shows
a tiny training loop swap. Update `docs/using-mlx.md` from
"design sketch" to "reference" and strip the `> Status: planned`
disclaimer; add a retrospective section summarizing what
actually shipped vs the sketch. Update `docs/status.md`
one-liner and `docs/saga.md` Saga 14 entry. Rebuild `pages/`
via `scripts/build-pages.sh` and commit both source and built
artifact in the same commit.

### Step 010 -- release v0.11.0
Bump workspace version to `0.11.0`. Release commit summarizes
`mlpl-mlx` runtime, `device("...") { }` scoped form,
`to_device(...)` movement helpers, autograd + optimizers on
MLX, and the Tiny LM MLX variant with its measured speedup.
Tag `v0.11.0`. Push commit and tag. Verify the pages workflow
deploys the updated tutorial list.

## Dependencies and risk

- Step 001 depends on `mlx-rs` building cleanly on the dev
  laptop; pin a known-good version in `Cargo.toml`. Risk: MLX
  crate ABI changes between versions. Mitigation: pin minor.
- Step 004 introduces `Expr::Device`. Risk: the scoped form
  interacts with `experiment { }` and `train { }` nesting.
  Mitigation: explicit tests for `experiment { device { train
  { } } }` and the swapped nesting.
- Step 006 is the highest-risk step. MLX's lazy graph and
  MLPL's tape are two autograd systems; we only get to pick
  one per expression. Decide during TDD whether to lean on
  `mlx-rs`'s `vjp` / `grad` wholesale or hand-write backward
  per primitive. Either path must produce the same gradients
  (within tolerance) as the CPU tape.
- Step 008 risk: MLX compile overhead on the first call may
  dominate the Tiny LM's wall clock at this size. Mitigation:
  bench harness reports both cold and warm timings; go/no-go
  is warm-path speedup.
- Cross-platform CI risk: every MLX test must be feature- and
  target-gated so `cargo test` on Linux CI stays green. This
  is an invariant across every step, not a single-step task.

=== STEPS ===
  [x] 001-mlx-crate-skeleton [production]: Phase 1 step 001: mlpl-mlx crate skeleton + first primitive.

Stand up the ML...
  [x] 002-mlx-elementwise-shape [production]: Phase 1 step 002: core elementwise + shape ops on MLX.

Fill out the forward-...
  [x] 003-mlx-reductions-softmax [production]: Phase 1 step 003: reductions + softmax + log-softmax on MLX.

Close the forwa...

Expose device ... <-- YOU ARE HERE
  [ ] 005-backend-dispatch-model [production]: Phase 2 step 005: backend dispatch in the Model DSL.

Make `apply`/`params` r...
  [ ] 006-mlx-autograd-tape [production]: Phase 3 step 006: tape-lowered ops on MLX + gradcheck parity.

Make `grad(exp...
  [ ] 007-mlx-optimizers-train [production]: Phase 3 step 007: optimizers + train { } on MLX.

Close the training loop on ...
  [ ] 008-tiny-lm-mlx-demo [production]: Phase 4 step 008: tiny LM MLX variant + bench harness.

Prove the speedup end...

Make Saga 14...

=== PROMPT ===
Phase 2 step 004: device("...") scoped form + parser surface.

Expose device placement in the MLPL source language.

1. Parser: new `Expr::Device { target: String, body:
   Box<Expr> }` AST node for `device("mlx") { body }` and
   `device("cpu") { body }`. Grammar mirrors `experiment
   "name" { body }`. Round-trip test: source -> parse ->
   pretty-print matches.
2. Evaluator: inside a `device("mlx") { ... }` block, array
   allocations and ops dispatch through `mlpl-mlx` when the
   `mlx` feature is compiled in. On non-MLX hosts, emit a
   one-time warning and fall back to the CPU runtime.
   `device("cpu") { ... }` is always a no-op (same values,
   same labels, same shapes) and must work on every host.
3. Nesting: a `device` block inside another `device` block
   uses the inner target. Test both directions of nesting
   with `experiment { device { ... } }` and `device {
   experiment { ... } }`.
4. Labels and shapes propagate across the block boundary
   unchanged -- a `[batch, feat]` label on a tensor allocated
   inside `device("mlx") { }` is still `[batch, feat]` when
   read outside.
5. TDD: parser test (red -> green -> refactor), then
   evaluator tests.
6. Quality gates + `/mw-cp`. Commit message references step 004.

=== WHEN DONE ===
Run: agentrail begin    (if not yet started)
Run: agentrail complete --summary "what you did" --next-slug <slug> --next-prompt "next instructions"
  Or: agentrail complete --summary "what you did" --done   (if this is the last step)

  between interpreter and compile-to-Rust paths on 9 programs.
- `mlpl-bench` Criterion harness comparing interpreter vs
  compiled MLPL (9.05x measured speedup on 100x100 reshape+
  reduce).
- `mlpl-lower-rs` path-configurable AST -> `TokenStream` codegen
  (the `runtime_prefix` knob makes swapping runtimes cheap).
- Full Saga 11/13 Model DSL, autograd, optimizers, and the
  Tiny LM end-to-end demo (`demos/tiny_lm.mlpl`).
- Labeled axes and structured `EvalError::ShapeMismatch`
  propagate through every existing op.

## Phase 1 -- mlpl-mlx runtime target (3 steps)

### Step 001 -- mlpl-mlx crate skeleton + first primitive
New `crates/mlpl-mlx` sibling to `mlpl-rt`. `mlx-rs` dependency
behind a `mlx` Cargo feature. Ports exactly one primitive --
`matmul(a, b)` -- backed by MLX's `matmul` and returning an
MLX array wrapped in the same `DenseArray` / `LabeledShape`
shape as `mlpl-rt`. Parity test vs `mlpl-rt::matmul` on a
`[8, 4] @ [4, 8]` fixture (bit-for-bit or within a documented
fp32 tolerance; decide during TDD). `cargo test -p mlpl-mlx
--features mlx` gates the test; default build stays green on
non-Apple hosts.

### Step 002 -- core elementwise + shape ops on MLX
Port the shape- and element-level primitives the existing
interpreter uses most: `add`/`sub`/`mul`/`div`/`neg`, `exp`/
`log`/`tanh`/`sigmoid`/`relu`, `reshape`, `transpose`. Each
primitive lands with a unit test + parity test vs `mlpl-rt`.
Labels propagate identically -- the MLX runtime does not own
`LabeledShape`, it borrows `mlpl-core`'s. Gradcheck is not in
scope yet (step 006).

### Step 003 -- reductions + softmax + log-softmax on MLX
`reduce_add`/`reduce_mul`/`mean`/`argmax` (axis-aware, label-
aware), `softmax`, `log_softmax`, and `cross_entropy`.
Numerical-stability invariants from the CPU path (max-
subtraction log-softmax) carry over. Parity tests on `[4, 3]`
and `[2, 3, 5]` fixtures. At the end of this phase the MLX
runtime can compute forward passes of every primitive the Tiny
LM uses.

## Phase 2 -- Device placement + backend dispatch (2 steps)

### Step 004 -- device("...") scoped form + parser surface
New `device("mlx") { body }` scoped form in the parser/AST
(`Expr::Device`), mirroring `experiment`'s shape. Inside the
block, evaluator dispatches array allocations and ops through
the MLX runtime when the `mlx` feature is enabled; falls back
to CPU (with a one-time warning) otherwise. Labels and shapes
propagate identically across the boundary. Tests: parser
round-trip, evaluator returns the same shape+labels as the CPU
path, `device("cpu") { }` is a no-op and works on every host.

### Step 005 -- backend dispatch in the Model DSL
`apply(model, X)` and `params(model)` route through the active
backend when `X` was allocated inside a `device("mlx")` block.
A model owns its parameters on one device -- moving between
devices requires an explicit `to_device("cpu")` / `to_device
("mlx")` builtin (single-direction helpers that copy tensors;
tests cover round-trip equality). The Saga 11 models
(`linear`, `chain`, `residual`, `rms_norm`, `attention`,
`causal_attention`) produce bit-for-bit (or tolerance-bounded)
equivalent outputs on both devices for fixed seeds.

## Phase 3 -- Autograd on MLX (2 steps)

### Step 006 -- tape-lowered ops on MLX + gradcheck parity
`grad(expr, wrt)` works inside a `device("mlx")` block. The
autograd tape's primitives (`add`, `mul`, `matmul`, `exp`,
`log`, `relu`, `tanh`, `sigmoid`, `softmax`, `sum`, `mean`,
`transpose`, `reshape`) all ship MLX-backed backward paths,
each gradcheck-verified against finite differences on fixtures
matching Saga 9's. Where MLX's own `vjp` / `grad` helps,
`mlpl-mlx` is free to use it; where it doesn't, we hand-write
the backward. Either way, the invariant is that
`grad(expr, wrt)` on MLX matches the CPU path to within a
documented tolerance.

### Step 007 -- optimizers + train { } on MLX
`adam`, `momentum_sgd`, `train N { body }`, `last_losses`,
`experiment "name" { }`, and the `_metric` capture all work
unchanged inside `device("mlx") { }`. The `OptimizerState` map
holds MLX-typed tensors when the surrounding scope is MLX.
Parity test: one Adam step on the Saga 11 `tiny_mlp` demo
produces the same parameter update (within tolerance) on CPU
and MLX.

## Phase 4 -- Tiny LM on MLX + benchmarks (1 step)

### Step 008 -- tiny LM MLX variant + bench harness
`demos/tiny_lm_mlx.mlpl` wraps the Saga 13 `demos/tiny_lm.mlpl`
body in `device("mlx") { ... }` and otherwise changes nothing.
Measurable outcome: identical-shape loss curve, >=5x wall-clock
speedup over the interpreter CPU path on an M-class laptop
(target 10-50x per `docs/using-mlx.md`, but 5x is the go / no-go
gate). Extend `mlpl-bench` with an MLX row on the existing
reshape+reduce workload and the Tiny LM training-step workload.
Document the numbers in `docs/benchmarks.md`.

## Phase 5 -- Docs + release (2 steps)

### Step 009 -- tutorial lesson + using-mlx.md retrospective
New web REPL tutorial lesson **"Running on MLX"** that walks
from a `device("cpu") { randn(7, [1024, 64]) }` baseline to the
same expression wrapped in `device("mlx") { ... }`, then shows
a tiny training loop swap. Update `docs/using-mlx.md` from
"design sketch" to "reference" and strip the `> Status: planned`
disclaimer; add a retrospective section summarizing what
actually shipped vs the sketch. Update `docs/status.md`
one-liner and `docs/saga.md` Saga 14 entry. Rebuild `pages/`
via `scripts/build-pages.sh` and commit both source and built
artifact in the same commit.

### Step 010 -- release v0.11.0
Bump workspace version to `0.11.0`. Release commit summarizes
`mlpl-mlx` runtime, `device("...") { }` scoped form,
`to_device(...)` movement helpers, autograd + optimizers on
MLX, and the Tiny LM MLX variant with its measured speedup.
Tag `v0.11.0`. Push commit and tag. Verify the pages workflow
deploys the updated tutorial list.

## Dependencies and risk

- Step 001 depends on `mlx-rs` building cleanly on the dev
  laptop; pin a known-good version in `Cargo.toml`. Risk: MLX
  crate ABI changes between versions. Mitigation: pin minor.
- Step 004 introduces `Expr::Device`. Risk: the scoped form
  interacts with `experiment { }` and `train { }` nesting.
  Mitigation: explicit tests for `experiment { device { train
  { } } }` and the swapped nesting.
- Step 006 is the highest-risk step. MLX's lazy graph and
  MLPL's tape are two autograd systems; we only get to pick
  one per expression. Decide during TDD whether to lean on
  `mlx-rs`'s `vjp` / `grad` wholesale or hand-write backward
  per primitive. Either path must produce the same gradients
  (within tolerance) as the CPU tape.
- Step 008 risk: MLX compile overhead on the first call may
  dominate the Tiny LM's wall clock at this size. Mitigation:
  bench harness reports both cold and warm timings; go/no-go
  is warm-path speedup.
- Cross-platform CI risk: every MLX test must be feature- and
  target-gated so `cargo test` on Linux CI stays green. This
  is an invariant across every step, not a single-step task.

1	//! AST node types for MLPL.
2	//!
3	//! These are parser-owned syntax nodes. They do NOT depend on mlpl-array.
4	
5	use mlpl_core::Span;
6	
7	/// Binary operator kind.
8	#[derive(Clone, Debug, PartialEq)]
9	pub enum BinOpKind {
10	    /// `+`
11	    Add,
12	    /// `-`
13	    Sub,
14	    /// `*`
15	    Mul,
16	    /// `/`
17	    Div,
18	}
19	
20	/// Kind of tensor constructor: trainable parameter or non-trainable tensor.
21	#[derive(Clone, Copy, Debug, PartialEq, Eq)]
22	pub enum TensorCtorKind {
23	    /// `param[shape]` -- trainable leaf (`requires_grad` = true).
24	    Param,
25	    /// `tensor[shape]` -- non-trainable leaf.
26	    Tensor,
27	}
28	
29	/// An expression in the MLPL AST.
30	#[derive(Clone, Debug, PartialEq)]
31	pub enum Expr {
32	    /// Integer literal.
33	    IntLit(i64, Span),
34	    /// Float literal.
35	    FloatLit(f64, Span),
36	    /// String literal.
37	    StrLit(String, Span),
38	    /// Identifier reference.
39	    Ident(String, Span),
40	    /// Array literal: `[expr, expr, ...]`
41	    ArrayLit(Vec<Expr>, Span),
42	    /// Binary operation: `lhs op rhs`
43	    BinOp {
44	        /// The operator.
45	        op: BinOpKind,
46	        /// Left-hand side.
47	        lhs: Box<Expr>,
48	        /// Right-hand side.
49	        rhs: Box<Expr>,
50	        /// Span covering the full expression.
51	        span: Span,
52	    },
53	    /// Function call: `name(args...)`
54	    FnCall {
55	        /// Function name.
56	        name: String,
57	        /// Arguments.
58	        args: Vec<Expr>,
59	        /// Span covering name through closing paren.
60	        span: Span,
61	    },
62	    /// Unary negation: `-expr`
63	    UnaryNeg {
64	        /// The operand.
65	        operand: Box<Expr>,
66	        /// Span covering the minus through the operand.
67	        span: Span,
68	    },
69	    /// Assignment: `name = value`
70	    Assign {
71	        /// Variable name.
72	        name: String,
73	        /// Value expression.
74	        value: Box<Expr>,
75	        /// Span covering name through value.
76	        span: Span,
77	    },
78	    /// Tensor constructor: `param[shape...]` or `tensor[shape...]`.
79	    TensorCtor {
80	        /// Which kind of leaf to construct.
81	        kind: TensorCtorKind,
82	        /// Shape dimension expressions.
83	        shape: Vec<Expr>,
84	        /// Span covering keyword through closing bracket.
85	        span: Span,
86	    },
87	    /// Repeat loop: `repeat <count> { body }`
88	    Repeat {
89	        /// Number of iterations.
90	        count: Box<Expr>,
91	        /// Body statements.
92	        body: Vec<Expr>,
93	        /// Span covering keyword through closing brace.
94	        span: Span,
95	    },
96	    /// Training loop: `train <count> { body }`. On each iteration the
97	    /// loop binds the iteration index to `step`, runs the body, and
98	    /// captures the value of the body's final statement as the
99	    /// per-step loss. After the loop, all captured losses are stored
100	    /// in the environment as a 1-D array under the name `last_losses`.
101	    Train {
102	        /// Number of training steps.
103	        count: Box<Expr>,
104	        /// Body statements; the value of the last one is the loss.
105	        body: Vec<Expr>,
106	        /// Span covering keyword through closing brace.
107	        span: Span,
108	    },
109	    /// Scoped experiment block: `experiment "name" { body }`
110	    /// (Saga 12 step 007). Runs body in the current environment;
111	    /// on exit, scans `_metric`-suffixed scalar vars and appends a
112	    /// record to `env.experiment_log`. When the environment has an
113	    /// `exp_dir` set (terminal REPL only), also writes a
114	    /// `run.json` record to disk.
115	    Experiment {
116	        /// Human-chosen name for the run; used in file paths.
117	        name: String,
118	        /// Body statements.
119	        body: Vec<Expr>,
120	        /// Span covering the keyword through the closing brace.
121	        span: Span,
122	    },
123	    /// Streaming iteration: `for <binding> in <source> { body }`
124	    /// (Saga 12 step 003). On each iteration binds `binding` to a
125	    /// rank-(r-1) slice of `source`'s axis 0. After the loop, each
126	    /// iteration's final value is captured into `last_rows` in the
127	    /// environment (mirrors `Train`'s `last_losses`).
128	    For {
129	        /// Name to bind to each row slice.
130	        binding: String,
131	        /// Source expression (must have rank >= 1).
132	        source: Box<Expr>,
133	        /// Body statements.
134	        body: Vec<Expr>,
135	        /// Span covering keyword through closing brace.
136	        span: Span,
137	    },
138	}
139	
140	impl Expr {
141	    /// Return the source span for this expression.
142	    #[must_use]
143	    pub fn span(&self) -> Span {
144	        match self {
145	            Self::IntLit(_, s)
146	            | Self::FloatLit(_, s)
147	            | Self::StrLit(_, s)
148	            | Self::Ident(_, s)
149	            | Self::ArrayLit(_, s)
150	            | Self::BinOp { span: s, .. }
151	            | Self::UnaryNeg { span: s, .. }
152	            | Self::FnCall { span: s, .. }
153	            | Self::Assign { span: s, .. }
154	            | Self::TensorCtor { span: s, .. }
155	            | Self::Repeat { span: s, .. }
156	            | Self::Train { span: s, .. }
157	            | Self::For { span: s, .. }
158	            | Self::Experiment { span: s, .. } => *s,
159	        }
160	    }
161	}
162

1	//! Token types for the MLPL lexer.
2	
3	use mlpl_core::Span;
4	
5	/// The kind of a lexer token.
6	#[derive(Clone, Debug, PartialEq)]
7	pub enum TokenKind {
8	    /// Integer literal.
9	    IntLit(i64),
10	    /// Float literal.
11	    FloatLit(f64),
12	    /// Identifier.
13	    Ident(String),
14	    /// String literal (double-quoted, escapes processed).
15	    StrLit(String),
16	    /// `(`
17	    LParen,
18	    /// `)`
19	    RParen,
20	    /// `[`
21	    LBracket,
22	    /// `]`
23	    RBracket,
24	    /// `,`
25	    Comma,
26	    /// `=`
27	    Equals,
28	    /// `:` (axis-label annotation, Saga 11.5 Phase 2).
29	    Colon,
30	    /// `;`
31	    Semicolon,
32	    /// `+`
33	    Plus,
34	    /// `-`
35	    Minus,
36	    /// `*`
37	    Star,
38	    /// `/`
39	    Slash,
40	    /// `{`
41	    LBrace,
42	    /// `}`
43	    RBrace,
44	    /// `repeat` keyword.
45	    Repeat,
46	    /// `train` keyword.
47	    Train,
48	    /// `for` keyword (streaming-iter, Saga 12 step 003).
49	    For,
50	    /// `in` keyword (only meaningful inside `for ... in ...`).
51	    In,
52	    /// `experiment` keyword (Saga 12 step 007).
53	    Experiment,
54	    /// Newline (statement separator).
55	    Newline,
56	    /// End of input.
57	    Eof,
58	}
59	
60	/// A token with its source span.
61	#[derive(Clone, Debug, PartialEq)]
62	pub struct Token {
63	    /// What kind of token.
64	    pub kind: TokenKind,
65	    /// Where in the source.
66	    pub span: Span,
67	}
68

1	//! Error types for mlpl-parser.
2	
3	use mlpl_core::Span;
4	
5	use crate::token::TokenKind;
6	
7	/// Render a `TokenKind` as the human-readable string used in
8	/// `ParseError::UnexpectedToken` messages. Lives in this module
9	/// because it is part of error surface formatting.
10	pub(crate) fn describe_kind(kind: &TokenKind) -> String {
11	    match kind {
12	        TokenKind::Eof => "end of input".into(),
13	        TokenKind::Newline => "newline".into(),
14	        TokenKind::IntLit(n) => format!("integer {n}"),
15	        TokenKind::FloatLit(n) => format!("float {n}"),
16	        TokenKind::StrLit(s) => format!("string \"{s}\""),
17	        TokenKind::Ident(s) => format!("identifier '{s}'"),
18	        TokenKind::LParen => "'('".into(),
19	        TokenKind::RParen => "')'".into(),
20	        TokenKind::LBracket => "'['".into(),
21	        TokenKind::RBracket => "']'".into(),
22	        TokenKind::LBrace => "'{'".into(),
23	        TokenKind::RBrace => "'}'".into(),
24	        TokenKind::Comma => "','".into(),
25	        TokenKind::Equals => "'='".into(),
26	        TokenKind::Colon => "':'".into(),
27	        TokenKind::Semicolon => "';'".into(),
28	        TokenKind::Plus => "'+'".into(),
29	        TokenKind::Minus => "'-'".into(),
30	        TokenKind::Star => "'*'".into(),
31	        TokenKind::Slash => "'/'".into(),
32	        TokenKind::Repeat => "'repeat'".into(),
33	        TokenKind::Train => "'train'".into(),
34	        TokenKind::For => "'for'".into(),
35	        TokenKind::In => "'in'".into(),
36	        TokenKind::Experiment => "'experiment'".into(),
37	    }
38	}
39	
40	/// Errors produced during lexing and parsing.
41	#[derive(Clone, Debug, PartialEq)]
42	pub enum ParseError {
43	    /// An unexpected character was encountered during lexing.
44	    UnexpectedCharacter {
45	        /// The character.
46	        ch: char,
47	        /// Where it was found.
48	        span: Span,
49	    },
50	    /// A number literal could not be parsed.
51	    InvalidNumber {
52	        /// Where the number was.
53	        span: Span,
54	    },
55	    /// An unexpected token was encountered during parsing.
56	    UnexpectedToken {
57	        /// Description of what was found.
58	        found: String,
59	        /// Where it was found.
60	        span: Span,
61	    },
62	    /// Expected a closing delimiter that was not found.
63	    UnclosedDelimiter {
64	        /// The opening delimiter.
65	        open: String,
66	        /// Where the opening delimiter was.
67	        span: Span,
68	    },
69	    /// Bytes inside a string literal were not valid UTF-8. Saga 12.
70	    InvalidUtf8 {
71	        /// Byte span of the offending sequence.

input.command:
git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
feat(mlpl-eval): Saga 14 step 004 -- device("...") scoped form

Expose device placement at the source level so MLPL programs can
opt into the MLX runtime per scope. The grammar mirrors
`experiment "name" { body }`: a `device` keyword, a string-literal
target inside parens, and a brace-delimited body.

Parser:
  - new `TokenKind::Device` keyword; lexer maps `device`
  - new `Expr::Device { target: String, body: Vec<Expr>, span }`
    AST variant; `Expr::span()` covers it
  - `parse_device` in `stmts.rs` parses `device(<str>) { body }`,
    dispatched from `parse_statement` like the other scoped forms
  - `mlpl-lower-rs` adds Device to its non-codegen Unsupported arm
    so compile-to-rust still builds; codegen support is deferred to
    a future step (it is feature-gated by design and will land with
    the per-target compile path)

Evaluator:
  - new `Environment::device_stack`, `push_device`, `pop_device`,
    and `device()` accessor (returns "cpu" by default). Stack
    means an inner `device("cpu")` block correctly overrides an
    outer `device("mlx")` and pops back on exit
  - new `mlpl-eval::device::eval_device` runs the body with the
    target pushed; mirrors `experiment`'s value-yielding shape
  - `Environment::take_mlx_fallback_warning()` flips a one-time
    bool so the "device(\"mlx\") requested but mlx feature not
    compiled in" warning fires at most once per `Environment`
  - `device::try_mlx_dispatch(name, args)` is the single place
    where eval routes through `mlpl-mlx`. Triple-gated on
    `feature = "mlx"` + `target_os = "macos"` +
    `target_arch = "aarch64"`; outside that combination the
    helper is a stub that always returns `None`, so eval falls
    back to the CPU runtime
  - `eval_binop` and `eval_fncall` consult `env.device()` and use
    the dispatch helper when on `"mlx"`; otherwise they take the
    existing CPU paths verbatim
  - `Expr::Device` match arm in `eval_expr` calls into the new
    module; `grad.rs` swaps its scoped-form arm list for a
    wildcard with a clarifying comment so adding Device to the AST
    doesn't push `eval_tensor_expr` over the 50-line ceiling

Cargo:
  - new `mlx` feature on `mlpl-eval` -- off by default --
    activates the optional `mlpl-mlx` dependency with its own
    `mlx` feature. Building `cargo test -p mlpl-eval --features
    mlx` on a non-Apple host still compiles (the `mlpl-mlx`
    triple-gate stubs out the surface) and the device block
    transparently falls back to CPU at run time

Tests (new `tests/device_block_tests.rs`):
  - parser: emits `Expr::Device` for `mlx`/`cpu`/unknown targets;
    rejects missing parens, non-string target, missing body
  - evaluator: `device("cpu") { body }` is a no-op (same
    shape/labels/values as the bare body); empty body returns
    scalar zero; assignments inside survive the block; a labeled
    tensor allocated inside is still labeled outside
  - nesting: `experiment { device { ... } }` records one run;
    `device { experiment { ... } }` records one run; an inner
    `device("cpu")` overrides an outer `device("mlx")`
  - mlx-feature-gated: `device("mlx") { matmul(a, b) }` matches
    the CPU path within fp32 tolerance, demonstrating that the
    scoped form actually routes through `mlpl-mlx`

Quality gates: workspace `cargo test` clean (default features);
`cargo test -p mlpl-eval --features mlx` + `cargo test -p
mlpl-mlx --features mlx` clean on Apple Silicon; clippy
`-D warnings` clean for both default and mlx-feature builds; fmt
clean; sw-checklist FAIL count unchanged from step 003 (135) --
no new failures introduced. Cross-platform CI invariant holds:
the default build never pulls in `mlpl-mlx`.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)"
